concepts.title: Saludos, despedidas y cortesía / Greetings, farewells and politeness
concepts.bridge: In English, we say 'hello', 'goodbye', 'thank you', 'please', 'excuse me', and 'I'm sorry'. In Spanish, it's similar: 'hola', 'adiós', 'gracias', 'por favor', 'perdón', 'lo siento'. The main differences are: 1) Spanish distinguishes between formal and informal greetings ('buenos días' vs 'hola'), and 2) 'Perdón' is used for minor apologies or to get attention, while 'lo siento' is for deeper regret. English speakers often use 'I'm sorry' for everything, so they need to learn when to use 'perdón' vs 'lo siento'.
concepts.rule: Use 'hola' for informal greetings, and 'buenos días/tardes/noches' for formal or time-specific greetings. For farewells, use 'adiós' (final/formal), 'hasta luego' (see you later), 'hasta mañana' (see you tomorrow). For politeness: 'gracias' (thank you), 'por favor' (please), 'perdón' (excuse me / I'm sorry for minor things), 'lo siento' (I'm sorry for serious things). Respond to 'gracias' with 'de nada'.

common_error.explanation: Using 'lo siento' (deep regret) for a minor apology or to get attention is incorrect. In Spanish, use 'perdón' or 'disculpa' for such situations. 'Lo siento' is reserved for expressing sympathy or regret for something serious.

examples.note: If you'll meet the next day.

examples.translation: Thank you very much for your help!
Excuse me, I didn't understand. Can you repeat?
examples.note: Using 'perdón' to get attention or apologize.

examples.original: Lo siento mucho, ha sido culpa mía.
examples.translation: I'm very sorry, it was my fault.
examples.note: Using 'lo siento' for a serious apology.

examples.translation: Hello, good morning, how are you? (formal)

examples.original: Hasta luego, que tengas buen día.
examples.translation: See you later, have a good day.
examples.note: Farewell with a wish using subjunctive 'que tengas'.

concepts.title: Números cardinales y ordinales / Cardinal and ordinal numbers
concepts.bridge: In English, numbers are similar: 'one, two, three, four, five...' In Spanish, they are 'uno, dos, tres, cuatro, cinco...'. The main differences are: 1) Spanish uses 'uno' before a masculine noun becomes 'un' (e.g., 'un libro'), and 'una' before feminine nouns (e.g., 'una casa'). 2) Numbers 16-19 are written as one word (dieciséis, diecisiete, etc.), unlike English where they are separate (sixteen, seventeen). 3) Ordinal numbers in Spanish agree in gender and number with the noun (primero/primera, primeros/primeras), while in English they are invariable. 4) The apocope of 'primero' and 'tercero' before a masculine singular noun: 'primer' and 'tercer' (e.g., 'el primer día').
concepts.rule: Cardinal numbers: 0 cero, 1 uno, 2 dos, 3 tres, 4 cuatro, 5 cinco, 6 seis, 7 siete, 8 ocho, 9 nueve, 10 diez, 11 once, 12 doce, 13 trece, 14 catorce, 15 quince, 16 dieciséis, 17 diecisiete, 18 dieciocho, 19 diecinueve, 20 veinte, 30 treinta, 40 cuarenta, 50 cincuenta, 60 sesenta, 70 setenta, 80 ochenta, 90 noventa, 100 cien (or ciento), 101 ciento uno, 200 doscientos, 1000 mil. Ordinal numbers: 1º primero (primer before masculine singular noun), 2º segundo, 3º tercero (tercer before masculine singular noun), 4º cuarto, 5º quinto, 6º sexto, 7º séptimo, 8º octavo, 9º noveno, 10º décimo.

common_error.explanation: The error is writing 'cinquenta' with a 'q' (influence from French 'cinquante' or English 'quarter'). In Spanish, it's always 'cincuenta' with a 'c'. Also, English speakers may say 'diez y seis' instead of 'dieciséis'.

examples.translation: sixty, seventy, eighty, ninety, one hundred
two hundred, three hundred, four hundred, five hundred
I live on the fifth floor.

examples.note: Apocope of 'primero' to 'primer' before masculine singular noun.

concepts.title: Colores, formas y tamaños / Colors, shapes and sizes
concepts.bridge: In English, colors are invariable (they don't change with gender or number). In Spanish, colors agree in gender and number with the noun they modify, except for a few that are invariable (e.g., 'azul', 'verde', 'gris', 'marrón', 'rosa'). For example: 'camisa roja' (red shirt, feminine), 'coche rojo' (red car, masculine). English speakers often forget to change the color adjective to match the noun's gender, saying 'camisa rojo' instead of 'roja'. Also, the position of adjectives: in English they go before the noun ('red car'), while in Spanish they usually go after ('coche rojo'). The exception is when an adjective is used for emphasis or with certain short adjectives like 'gran' (apocope of 'grande' before a singular noun).
concepts.rule: Colors: rojo/roja, azul (invariable), verde (invariable), amarillo/amarilla, negro/negra, blanco/blanca, gris (invariable), marrón (invariable), rosa (invariable), naranja (or anaranjado/anaranjada). Shapes: círculo (m), cuadrado (m), triángulo (m), rectángulo (m). Sizes: grande (apocope 'gran' before masculine singular nouns), pequeño/pequeña, mediano/mediana, largo/larga, corto/corta. The adjective usually goes after the noun.

common_error.explanation: The error is not matching the color to the noun's gender. 'Camisa' is feminine, so the adjective must be feminine: 'roja'. English speakers are used to colors being invariable, so they forget to change the ending.

examples.original: La casa es grande y blanca.
examples.translation: The house is big and white.
examples.note: Size + color (agreement in gender and number).

examples.original: Mi coche es más pequeño que el tuyo.
examples.translation: My car is smaller than yours.

examples.original: Prefiero el color rosa al naranja.
La mesa es rectangular y marrón.
examples.translation: The table is rectangular and brown.

examples.note: Apocope of 'grande' to 'gran' before masculine singular noun.

concepts.title: Días, meses y estaciones / Days, months and seasons
concepts.bridge: In English, days of the week and months are capitalized (Monday, January), but in Spanish they are not (lunes, enero). English speakers often capitalize them out of habit. The days of the week are similar but different: 'Monday' is 'lunes', 'Tuesday' is 'martes', etc. Months are also similar: 'January' is 'enero', 'February' is 'febrero', etc. Seasons: 'spring' is 'primavera', 'summer' is 'verano', 'autumn' is 'otoño', 'winter' is 'invierno'. The main difficulty is the accent on 'miércoles' (Wednesday) and 'sábado' (Saturday), and the spelling of 'septiembre' (September) which English speakers might spell with a 't' (like 'September') but in Spanish it's 'septiembre' or 'setiembre' without the 't'. Also, the preposition used with days: in English we say 'on Monday', but in Spanish it's 'el lunes' (with the article) and no preposition. For months, we use 'en' + month (e.g., 'en enero').
concepts.rule: Days: lunes, martes, miércoles (with accent), jueves, viernes, sábado (with accent), domingo. All are masculine. Months: enero, febrero, marzo, abril, mayo, junio, julio, agosto, septiembre, octubre, noviembre, diciembre. Seasons: primavera, verano, otoño (with accent), invierno. Use 'el' before days (e.g., 'el lunes') for 'on Monday'. Use 'en' for months and seasons (e.g., 'en enero', 'en verano').

common_error.explanation: Two errors: 1) Capitalizing the day (in Spanish, days are not capitalized). 2) Missing the accent on 'miércoles'. English speakers often forget to use accents and incorrectly capitalize days and months because they are used to doing so in English.

examples.translation: On Monday I have a meeting.

examples.original: Mi cumpleaños es el 15 de agosto.

concepts.title: La familia / The family
concepts.bridge: In English, we say 'father, mother, brother, sister, grandfather, grandmother, uncle, aunt, cousin'. In Spanish, it's very similar: 'padre, madre, hermano, hermana, abuelo, abuela, tío, tía, primo, prima'. The main differences are: 1) Spanish has a distinction between 'padres' (parents) and 'parientes' (relatives). English speakers often use 'parents' for both, but in Spanish 'padres' only means 'father and mother'. 2) Spanish has grammatical gender for all family members, including 'primo' (male cousin) vs 'prima' (female cousin), while English uses 'cousin' for both. 3) The possessive adjectives in Spanish agree with the noun (mi, tu, su, nuestro), similar to English, but English speakers sometimes forget to use the article with possessive adjectives (e.g., 'el mi casa' is incorrect, just 'mi casa').
concepts.rule: Family members: padre (father), madre (mother), hijo/hija (son/daughter), hermano/hermana (brother/sister), abuelo/abuela (grandfather/grandmother), tío/tía (uncle/aunt), primo/prima (cousin), sobrino/sobrina (nephew/niece), cuñado/cuñada (brother-in-law/sister-in-law), suegro/suegra (father-in-law/mother-in-law). To say 'parents' (mother and father), use 'padres' (plural masculine). To say 'relatives' (extended family), use 'parientes'. Possessive adjectives: mi (my), tu (your, informal), su (his/her/your formal/their), nuestro (our), vuestro (your plural, Spain). They go before the noun and agree in number (but not gender for the possessive itself: mi/mis, tu/tus, su/sus).

common_error.correct_alt: Mis parientes viven en Francia. (if you mean extended family)
common_error.explanation: The error is using 'parentes' (relatives) to mean 'parents' (father and mother). In Spanish, 'padres' means 'parents' (the couple), while 'parientes' means 'relatives' (extended family). English speakers often confuse 'parents' with 'relatives' because in English 'parents' is only the immediate couple, but they may think 'parentes' sounds similar to 'parents' and use it incorrectly.

examples.original: Mi hermana es mayor que yo.
examples.translation: My sister is older than me.

examples.original: Tengo dos primos y una prima.
examples.translation: I have two cousins (male) and one cousin (female).

examples.note: familia numerosa = large family (with many children).

concepts.title: La casa / The house
concepts.bridge: In English, we say 'house, apartment, rooms (living room, bedroom, kitchen, bathroom), furniture (table, chair, bed, closet, sofa), appliances (refrigerator, washing machine, oven)'. In Spanish, it's similar: 'casa, piso, salón (or sala), dormitorio (or habitación), cocina, baño, comedor, garaje, jardín, mesa, silla, cama, armario, sofá, nevera (or frigorífico), lavadora, horno'. The main difficulties for English speakers are: 1) The gender of nouns: 'el baño' (masculine) vs 'la cocina' (feminine) – English speakers often use the wrong article because they think of the English word's gender (e.g., 'bathroom' is neuter in English, so they might say 'la baño' incorrectly). 2) The position of adjectives: in English we say 'big house', but in Spanish it's 'casa grande' (adjective after noun). 3) The use of 'en' for location (e.g., 'en la cocina') vs 'en' also used for 'in' – it's similar to English, but English speakers sometimes confuse 'sobre' (on) with 'en' (in/on).
concepts.rule: Rooms: salón (or sala), cocina, dormitorio (or habitación), baño, comedor, garaje, jardín, pasillo. Furniture: mesa, silla, cama, armario, sofá, estantería, escritorio. Appliances: nevera (or frigorífico), lavadora, horno, microondas, lavavajillas. Gender: 'el baño' (m), 'la cocina' (f), 'el dormitorio' (m), 'la habitación' (f). For location, use 'estar' (not 'ser') + 'en' (e.g., 'La mesa está en la cocina').

common_error.explanation: The error is using the feminine article 'la' with 'baño', which is masculine. English speakers don't have grammatical gender for inanimate objects, so they often guess the gender based on the English word or random chance. 'Baño' is masculine, so it's 'el baño'.

examples.original: La mesa está en el comedor.
examples.translation: The table is in the dining room.

examples.original: Pongo la ropa en la lavadora.
examples.translation: I put the clothes in the washing machine.

examples.original: La nevera está llena de comida.
examples.translation: The refrigerator is full of food.

examples.original: Vivo en un piso con jardín.
examples.translation: I live in an apartment with a garden.

concepts.title: La ciudad / The city
concepts.bridge: In English, we say 'street, square, supermarket, pharmacy, bank, park, cinema, restaurant, hospital, school, train station, airport'. In Spanish, it's similar: 'calle, plaza, supermercado, farmacia, banco, parque, cine, restaurante, hospital, escuela, estación, aeropuerto'. The main difficulties for English speakers are: 1) The word 'cine' (cinema) vs 'cinema' – English speakers often say 'cinema' because it's the English word, but in Spanish it's 'cine'. 2) The gender of 'estación' (feminine) and 'aeropuerto' (masculine) – English speakers may use the wrong article. 3) The preposition 'a' for direction ('voy al cine') vs 'en' for location ('estoy en el cine') – English speakers may confuse 'to' and 'in'. Also, the use of 'estar' for location ('el banco está en la calle Mayor') – English speakers might use 'ser' incorrectly because in English we say 'the bank is' with 'to be'.
concepts.rule: Places: calle (f), plaza (f), supermercado (m), farmacia (f), banco (m), parque (m), cine (m), restaurante (m), hospital (m), escuela (f), iglesia (f), museo (m), estación (f), aeropuerto (m). Use 'estar' for location (e.g., '¿Dónde está el banco?'). Use 'ir a' for going to a place (e.g., 'Voy al cine').

common_error.wrong: Voy al cinema con mis amigos.
common_error.correct: Voy al cine con mis amigos.
common_error.explanation: The error is using 'cinema' instead of 'cine'. In Spanish, the place is called 'cine' (from 'cinematógrafo'), not 'cinema'. English speakers often use the English word because it's more familiar, but it's incorrect in Spanish.

examples.original: Voy al supermercado a comprar pan.
examples.translation: I go to the supermarket to buy bread.

examples.original: El hospital está cerca de la plaza.
examples.translation: The hospital is near the square.

examples.original: La farmacia está a la derecha del banco.
examples.translation: The pharmacy is to the right of the bank.

examples.original: El cine está en la calle Mayor.
examples.translation: The cinema is on Main Street.

concepts.title: Profesiones / Professions
concepts.bridge: In English, we say 'teacher, doctor, nurse, engineer, student, cook, driver, lawyer, architect, journalist, firefighter, police officer, pilot, flight attendant'. In Spanish, it's very similar: 'profesor, médico, enfermero, ingeniero, estudiante, cocinero, conductor, abogado, arquitecto, periodista, bombero, policía, piloto, azafato/azafata'. The main differences are: 1) In Spanish, professions are not preceded by an article after the verb 'ser' (e.g., 'Soy profesor' – not 'Soy un profesor'). English speakers often add the article because in English we say 'I am a teacher'. 2) Many professions change to feminine by changing -o to -a (e.g., 'enfermero/enfermera'), but some are invariable (e.g., 'periodista', 'policía'). 3) The word 'conductor' is used for 'driver', not 'motorista' (which is less common). 4) English speakers may confuse 'abogado' with 'advocate' (false friend) – 'abogado' means 'lawyer'.
concepts.rule: Professions: profesor, profesora (teacher), médico, médica (doctor), enfermero, enfermera (nurse), ingeniero, ingeniera (engineer), estudiante (student, invariable), cocinero, cocinera (cook), conductor, conductora (driver), abogado, abogada (lawyer), arquitecto, arquitecta (architect), periodista (journalist, invariable), bombero, bombera (firefighter), policía (police officer, invariable), piloto (pilot, invariable), azafato, azafata (flight attendant). No article after 'ser': 'Soy profesor' (not 'Soy un profesor').

common_error.explanation: The error is adding the indefinite article 'un' before the profession after 'ser'. In English, we say 'I am a teacher', but in Spanish, the article is omitted when stating a profession with the verb 'ser'. This is a very common mistake for English speakers.

examples.translation: What do you do? - I'm a student.

examples.original: El periodista trabaja en la radio.
examples.translation: The journalist works on the radio.
examples.note: Invariable in gender ('periodista' is both masculine and feminine).

concepts.title: Ropa y accesorios / Clothing and accessories
concepts.bridge: In English, we say 'shirt, pants, dress, shoes, jacket, coat, sweater, socks, hat, belt, bag, tie, glasses, watch, bracelet'. In Spanish, it's similar: 'camisa, pantalón, vestido, zapatos, chaqueta, abrigo, jersey, calcetines, sombrero, cinturón, bolso, corbata, gafas, reloj, pulsera'. The main difficulties for English speakers are: 1) The gender of some words: 'el pantalón' (masculine) – English speakers might say 'la pantalón' because 'pants' are plural in English. 2) The accent on 'pantalón' and 'cinturón' (they are written with an accent). 3) The plural of 'calcetín' is 'calcetines' – English speakers might say 'calcetines' correctly but may forget the accent. 4) 'Gafas' is always plural in Spanish, even though in English we say 'glasses' (also plural). 5) The word 'bolso' for 'bag' vs 'bolsa' (which means 'plastic bag') – English speakers might confuse them.
concepts.rule: Clothing: camisa (f), pantalón (m), vestido (m), falda (f), chaqueta (f), abrigo (m), jersey (m), camiseta (f), calcetines (m.pl), zapatos (m.pl), botas (f.pl). Accessories: sombrero (m), cinturón (m), bolso (m), corbata (f), gafas (f.pl), reloj (m), pulsera (f). For wearing something, use 'llevar' (e.g., 'Llevo una chaqueta negra').

common_error.explanation: The error is missing the accent on 'pantalón'. In Spanish, words ending in -n, -s, or a vowel that are stressed on the last syllable must have an accent. 'Pantalón' is stressed on the 'o', so it needs an accent. English speakers often forget accents because they don't exist in English.

examples.translation: I want to try on this shirt.
Do you have this skirt in size M?

concepts.title: Comida y bebida / Food and drink
concepts.bridge: In English, we say 'bread, rice, pasta, meat, fish, fruit, vegetables, eggs, cheese, yogurt' and 'water, juice, coffee, tea, milk, beer, wine'. In Spanish, it's similar: 'pan, arroz, pasta, carne, pescado, frutas, verduras, huevos, queso, yogur' and 'agua, zumo (or jugo), café, té, leche, cerveza, vino'. The main difficulties for English speakers are: 1) The gender of 'agua' (feminine, but uses 'el' in singular because it starts with stressed 'a' – 'el agua', but 'las aguas'). English speakers often say 'la agua' (incorrect). 2) 'Zumo' is used in Spain, 'jugo' in Latin America – English speakers might not know which one to use. 3) The use of the partitive article doesn't exist in Spanish: in English we say 'I drink water', in Spanish it's 'Bebo agua' (no article). 4) Plural forms of food items: 'frutas' vs 'fruta' (fruit as a category is 'fruta', but plural for multiple pieces).
concepts.rule: Food: pan (m), arroz (m), pasta (f), carne (f), pescado (m), fruta (f), verdura (f), huevo (m), queso (m), yogur (m). Drinks: agua (f, but 'el agua' and 'un agua'), zumo (m, Spain) / jugo (m, Latin America), café (m), té (m), leche (f), cerveza (f), vino (m). For general statements, use no article: 'Como pan' (I eat bread), 'Bebo agua' (I drink water). For preferences, use the definite article: 'Me gusta el pan' (I like bread).

common_error.explanation: The error is using the partitive article 'del' (de + el) which doesn't exist in Spanish. In English, we say 'I drink water' without an article. In Spanish, you also don't use an article for general amounts. The partitive article is not used in Spanish for quantities of food or drink.

examples.translation: I buy a kilo of apples.
I want a coffee with milk.

examples.original: La paella es un plato típico español.
examples.translation: Paella is a typical Spanish dish.

concepts.title: Animales domésticos y de granja / Pets and farm animals
concepts.bridge: In English, we say 'dog, cat, cow, horse, chicken, pig, sheep, duck, rabbit, goat'. In Spanish, it's similar: 'perro, gato, vaca, caballo, gallina, cerdo, oveja, pato, conejo, cabra'. The main difficulties for English speakers are: 1) The difference between 'perro' (dog) and 'cachorro' (puppy). English speakers often use 'puppy' for a young dog, but in Spanish, 'cachorro' means 'puppy' (or cub), not the adult dog. 2) The gender of some animals: 'el caballo' (horse) is masculine, even though in English it's neuter. 3) The plural forms: 'los perros' (the dogs) – English speakers might forget to add the -s. 4) Some false friends: 'cerdo' (pig) is similar to 'cider'? No, but 'puerco' is also used in some regions. The most common error is using 'cachorro' for an adult dog.
concepts.rule: Pets: perro (m), gato (m), conejo (m), hámster (m), pez (m). Farm animals: vaca (f), caballo (m), oveja (f), cerdo (m), gallina (f), pato (m), cabra (f), burro (m). For the species, we often use the masculine gender (e.g., 'el perro' for the species), but for the female animal, we use the feminine form ('la perra' for a female dog).

common_error.explanation: The error is using 'cachorro' (puppy) to describe an adult dog. In Spanish, 'cachorro' means 'puppy' or 'cub' (young animal). For an adult dog, you should say 'perro' (or 'perra' for a female dog). English speakers may confuse this because they think 'cachorro' sounds similar to 'cocker' or they associate it with any dog, but it specifically means a young one.

examples.original: El perro es grande y marrón.
examples.translation: The dog is big and brown.

examples.original: Mi gato duerme en la cama.
examples.translation: My cat sleeps on the bed.

examples.original: Tengo un perro y dos gatos.
examples.translation: I have a dog and two cats.

concepts.title: El cuerpo humano / The human body
concepts.bridge: In English, we say 'head, arms, legs, eyes, mouth, nose, ears, hands, feet'. In Spanish, it's similar: 'cabeza, brazos, piernas, ojos, boca, nariz, orejas, manos, pies'. The main difficulties for English speakers are: 1) The gender of some body parts: 'la mano' (hand) is feminine, but English speakers might think it's masculine because it ends in -o. 'el pie' (foot) is masculine. 'la nariz' (nose) is feminine. 2) The plural of 'pie' is 'pies' (which is also a false friend with 'pies' in English). 3) The use of 'los ojos' (eyes) and 'las orejas' (ears) – English speakers might use the singular incorrectly. 4) The expression 'me duele' (it hurts me) is used differently in Spanish: instead of 'my head hurts', you say 'me duele la cabeza' (literally, 'the head hurts me'). English speakers might say 'mi cabeza duele' (incorrect).
concepts.rule: Body parts: cabeza (f), cara (f), ojos (m), nariz (f), boca (f), orejas (f), cuello (m), hombros (m), brazos (m), manos (f), dedos (m), pecho (m), espalda (f), piernas (f), rodillas (f), pies (m). To say 'I have a headache', use 'Me duele la cabeza' (not 'Mi cabeza duele'). The possessive adjective is not used; instead, use the definite article + the body part. The indirect object pronoun (me, te, le, etc.) indicates who it hurts.

common_error.explanation: The error is using the masculine article 'el' with 'nariz', which is feminine. English speakers often guess the gender of nouns based on the word's ending, but 'nariz' ends in -z and is feminine. They might think it's masculine because it ends in a consonant, but it's actually feminine.

examples.original: Tengo el pelo largo y los ojos azules.
examples.translation: I have long hair and blue eyes.

examples.original: Lávate las manos antes de comer.
Se rompió la pierna jugando al fútbol.
examples.translation: He broke his leg playing soccer.

concepts.title: Verbos de acción cotidianos / Everyday action verbs
concepts.bridge: In English, we say 'get up, shower, get dressed, have breakfast, go to work, work, have lunch, study, exercise, have dinner, go to bed, sleep'. In Spanish, it's similar: 'levantarse, ducharse, vestirse, desayunar, ir al trabajo, trabajar, almorzar, estudiar, hacer deporte, cenar, acostarse, dormir'. The main difficulties for English speakers are: 1) Reflexive verbs: Spanish uses reflexive pronouns (me, te, se, nos, os, se) that must agree with the subject. English speakers often forget these (e.g., 'yo levanto' instead of 'me levanto'). 2) The word order: in Spanish, the reflexive pronoun goes before the verb (me levanto), while in English, it's after the verb (I get up). 3) Some verbs have irregular conjugations in the present tense (e.g., almorzar, acostarse, dormir). 4) English speakers often confuse 'almorzar' (to have lunch) with 'comer' (to eat) or 'cenar' (to have dinner).
concepts.rule: Everyday verbs: levantarse (to get up), ducharse (to shower), lavarse (to wash), vestirse (to get dressed), desayunar (to have breakfast), ir al trabajo (to go to work), trabajar (to work), almorzar (to have lunch, o→ue), estudiar (to study), hacer deporte (to exercise), cenar (to have dinner), acostarse (to go to bed, o→ue), dormir (to sleep, o→ue). Reflexive verbs: the pronoun must agree with the subject: me (yo), te (tú), se (él/ella/usted), nos (nosotros), os (vosotros), se (ellos/ellas/ustedes).

common_error.explanation: The error is using the wrong reflexive pronoun ('se') for 'yo' and the wrong conjugation ('levanta'). In Spanish, the reflexive pronoun must match the subject: 'yo' uses 'me', not 'se'. Also, the verb must be conjugated correctly: 'levanto' for 'yo'. English speakers often confuse reflexive pronouns because they think of 'oneself' or use 'se' as a generic reflexive.

examples.original: Me levanto, me ducho y desayuno.
examples.translation: I get up, shower, and have breakfast.

examples.original: Me acuesto tarde los fines de semana.
examples.translation: I go to bed late on weekends.

concepts.title: Adjetivos descriptivos / Descriptive adjectives
concepts.bridge: In English, we say 'big, small, beautiful, ugly, young, old, new, happy, sad, intelligent, friendly, fun, easy, difficult, expensive, cheap, fast, slow'. In Spanish, it's similar: 'grande, pequeño, bonito, feo, joven, viejo, nuevo, alegre, triste, inteligente, simpático, divertido, fácil, difícil, caro, barato, rápido, lento'. The main difficulties for English speakers are: 1) Gender and number agreement: In Spanish, adjectives must agree in gender and number with the noun (e.g., 'casa bonita', 'coches bonitos'). English speakers often forget to change the ending (e.g., saying 'casa bonito' instead of 'bonita'). 2) Position: In English, adjectives go before the noun ('red car'), but in Spanish, they usually go after ('coche rojo'). 3) Apocope: Some adjectives shorten before masculine singular nouns: 'grande' becomes 'gran', 'bueno' becomes 'buen', 'malo' becomes 'mal', 'primero' becomes 'primer', 'tercero' becomes 'tercer'. English speakers often don't know these apocopes and use the full form incorrectly.
concepts.rule: Adjectives agree in gender (masculine/feminine) and number (singular/plural) with the noun. Examples: 'casa bonita' (f.sg), 'casas bonitas' (f.pl), 'hombre alto' (m.sg), 'hombres altos' (m.pl). Adjectives ending in -e or a consonant are usually invariable in gender (e.g., 'inteligente', 'joven'). Position: usually after the noun ('coche rojo'), but some short adjectives can go before ('buen día', 'mal tiempo', 'gran casa' – apocope). Important apocopes: grande → gran (before singular nouns), bueno → buen (before masculine singular), malo → mal (before masculine singular), primero → primer, tercero → tercer.

common_error.explanation: The error is not matching the adjective to the noun's gender. 'Casa' is feminine, so the adjective must be feminine: 'bonita'. English speakers often forget to change the adjective ending because English adjectives are invariable.

examples.original: Mi coche es más rápido que el tuyo.
examples.translation: My car is faster than yours.

examples.note: Apocope of 'grande' before a singular noun.

concepts.title: Adverbios de frecuencia / Adverbs of frequency
concepts.bridge: In English, we say 'always, almost always, often, sometimes, occasionally, rarely, never'. In Spanish, it's similar: 'siempre, casi siempre, a menudo, a veces, de vez en cuando, raramente (or rara vez), nunca'. The main difficulty for English speakers is the position of the adverb in the sentence. In English, adverbs of frequency usually go before the main verb (e.g., 'I always eat fruit'), but they can also go after 'to be' (e.g., 'I am always late'). In Spanish, the adverb usually goes before the main verb (e.g., 'Siempre como fruta'), but it can also go at the beginning or end for emphasis. English speakers often place the adverb after the verb (e.g., 'Como siempre fruta'), which is incorrect. Also, English speakers may confuse 'a menudo' (often) with 'a veces' (sometimes). The negative adverb 'nunca' (never) can be used alone before the verb, or with 'no' after the verb for emphasis ('no... nunca').
concepts.rule: Adverbs of frequency: siempre (always), casi siempre (almost always), a menudo (often), a veces (sometimes), de vez en cuando (occasionally), raramente / rara vez (rarely), nunca (never). Position: usually before the main verb (e.g., 'Siempre estudio'). For compound tenses, it goes between the auxiliary and the participle (e.g., 'He siempre estudiado'). It can be placed at the beginning or end of the sentence for emphasis, but the most natural position is before the verb. With 'nunca', it can be used alone before the verb ('Nunca como carne') or with 'no' after the verb for emphasis ('No como carne nunca').

common_error.wrong: Yo estudio siempre en la biblioteca.
common_error.correct: Yo siempre estudio en la biblioteca.
common_error.explanation: The error is placing 'siempre' after the verb, as in English ('I always study' → 'Yo estudio siempre'). In Spanish, the natural position for adverbs of frequency is before the main verb (except for emphasis). English speakers often carry over the English word order.

examples.translation: I always have breakfast at home.

examples.original: A veces voy al cine los sábados.
examples.translation: Sometimes I go to the cinema on Saturdays.
examples.note: At the beginning of the sentence.

examples.original: ¿Con qué frecuencia estudias español? - Estudio todos los días.
examples.translation: How often do you study Spanish? - I study every day.

concepts.title: Expresiones de tiempo / Time expressions
concepts.bridge: In English, we say 'today, tomorrow, yesterday, now, later, before, soon, early, late, in the morning, in the afternoon, in the evening'. In Spanish, it's similar: 'hoy, mañana, ayer, ahora, después, antes, luego, pronto, tarde, temprano, por la mañana, por la tarde, por la noche'. The main difficulties for English speakers are: 1) The word 'mañana' means both 'tomorrow' and 'morning' (as a noun). English speakers may confuse it with 'morning' and use it incorrectly (e.g., 'mañana por la mañana' = tomorrow morning is correct, but using 'mañana' alone for 'morning' without context can be ambiguous). 2) The prepositions for parts of the day: in English we say 'in the morning', but in Spanish it's 'por la mañana' (not 'en la mañana', though that is also used in some regions). 3) The position of time expressions: in English, time expressions often come at the end or beginning of the sentence; in Spanish, they are more flexible but usually follow the verb. 4) False friend: 'después' means 'after' or 'later', not 'since' (which is 'desde').
concepts.rule: Absolute time: hoy (today), mañana (tomorrow), ayer (yesterday), ahora (now), después (later/after), antes (before), luego (later), pronto (soon), tarde (late), temprano (early). Parts of the day: por la mañana (in the morning), por la tarde (in the afternoon), por la noche (in the evening / at night). Also: al mediodía (at noon), a medianoche (at midnight).

common_error.correct: Voy al cine por la noche.
common_error.correct_alt: Voy al cine de noche (colloquial, but less standard).
common_error.explanation: The error is using 'de noche' instead of 'por la noche' for the time of day. In Spanish, the standard expression for 'at night' when referring to an activity is 'por la noche'. 'De noche' is used in expressions like 'de noche' (nocturnal) but is less common for indicating when something happens. English speakers might translate 'at night' literally as 'de noche'.

examples.note: Part of the day with 'por la'.

examples.original: Salgo a pasear por la tarde.
examples.translation: I go for a walk in the afternoon.

examples.original: Primero desayuno, luego trabajo y después ceno.
examples.translation: First I have breakfast, then I work, and later I have dinner.

examples.original: Mañana por la mañana tengo una cita.
examples.translation: Tomorrow morning I have an appointment.
I will arrive soon, don't be late.
